title: Mancunian Films

infobox.value: was a British film production company first organised in 1933. From 1947 it was based in [Rusholme](/wiki/Rusholme "Rusholme"), a suburb of [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), and produced a number of comedy films, mostly aimed at audiences in the [North of England](/wiki/North_of_England "North of England").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British film production company

Mancunian Films

|  |  |

**Mancunian Films** was a British film production company first organised in 1933. From 1947 it was based in [Rusholme](/wiki/Rusholme "Rusholme"), a suburb of [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"), and produced a number of comedy films, mostly aimed at audiences in the [North of England](/wiki/North_of_England "North of England").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mancunian_Films&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Founded by [John E. Blakeley](/wiki/John_E._Blakeley "John E. Blakeley"), the company produced films in [London](/wiki/London "London") on extremely low-budgets. From property records at HM Land Registry, on 17 June 1933 Blakeley's Productions Limited, 148 Slade Lane, Levenshulme in Manchester bought [Hulme Hippodrome](/wiki/Hulme_Hippodrome "Hulme Hippodrome") for £17,900, selling it on 23 December 1935. Blakeley's first studio consisted of a single soundstage in a loft space in London above a taxi garage. Whenever the filmmakers wanted to shoot a scene, they would first have to signal the mechanics below to stop working, so the noise from below wouldn't register on the soundtracks. Blakeley's first production was *[Boots! Boots!](/wiki/Boots!_Boots! "Boots! Boots!")* (1934), starring the variety entertainer [George Formby](/wiki/George_Formby "George Formby") in his first released film.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Production values were so low that some scenes were filmed in semi-darkness, to hide the lack of set decorations. Despite the limitations, the debut film was a huge success in the regions, recouping Blakeley's investment several times over and launching George Formby as Britain's leading screen comedian. Within the year "Blakeley's Productions, Ltd." had become "The Mancunian Film Distributors, Ltd". Blakeley initially used facilities like [Riverside Studios](/wiki/Riverside_Studios#Film_studio "Riverside Studios"); the films were released via [Butcher's Films](/wiki/Butcher%27s_Film_Service "Butcher's Film Service").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1997276-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Dickenson Road Studios

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mancunian_Films&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Dickenson Road Studios")]

Main article: [Dickenson Road Studios](/wiki/Dickenson_Road_Studios "Dickenson Road Studios")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Where_TOTP_used_to_be_filmed_%28469064895%29.jpg/250px-Where_TOTP_used_to_be_filmed_%28469064895%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Where_TOTP_used_to_be_filmed_(469064895).jpg)

A plaque on a house wall marks the former site of Dickenson Road Studios

Escalating costs and a desire to cater for the robust tastes of northern industrial audiences led to the establishment of the two-stage facility[[4]](#cite_note-4) at [Dickenson Road Studios](/wiki/Dickenson_Road_Studios "Dickenson Road Studios"), a former Methodist Chapel on Dickenson Road in [Rusholme](/wiki/Rusholme "Rusholme"), the only film studio outside the South East.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1997276-2) The buildings were converted at a cost of £70,000 in 1947,[[5]](#cite_note-BBC20030217-5) with funding from the [National Film Finance Corporation](/wiki/National_Film_Finance_Corporation "National Film Finance Corporation") (NFFC), which provided grants to support independent British studios.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunterPorter201260-6)

Beginning with *[Cup-tie Honeymoon](/wiki/Cup-tie_Honeymoon "Cup-tie Honeymoon")* (1948) starring [Sandy Powell](/wiki/Sandy_Powell_(comedian) "Sandy Powell (comedian)"), over the next six years the films went on to feature northern favourites [Frank Randle](/wiki/Frank_Randle "Frank Randle"), [Josef Locke](/wiki/Josef_Locke "Josef Locke"), [Diana Dors](/wiki/Diana_Dors "Diana Dors"), and [Jimmy Clitheroe](/wiki/Jimmy_Clitheroe "Jimmy Clitheroe"). The Mancunian Films production operation earned the nickname "the [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") of the North",[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1997276-2) or alternatively "Jollywood", on account of its output of comedy films. Critics of Mancunian's productions dubbed the studio the "Corn Exchange", a humorous reference to the [Corn Exchange](/wiki/Corn_Exchange,_Manchester "Corn Exchange, Manchester") in Manchester (*"[corn](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/corn "wikt:corn")"* being a slang term for unoriginal, poor-quality humour).[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunterPorter201260-6)

The studio, often working on shoestring budgets, was profitable. The [cinematographic](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography") expertise developed in Manchester formed the foundations of [Granada Television](/wiki/ITV_Granada "ITV Granada").

In 1950, the NFFC demanded that Blakeley repay its £50,000 loan. The NFFC chairman, [Lord Reith](/wiki/John_Reith,_1st_Baron_Reith "John Reith, 1st Baron Reith"), expressed dissatisfaction with the quality of Mancunian's comedy productions; of *[Over the Garden Wall](/wiki/Over_the_Garden_Wall_(1950_film) "Over the Garden Wall (1950 film)")* (1950), Reith said it was not "of as high a quality as the Corporation would have wished". This created a funding crisis for the studio, but Blakeley managed to raise the necessary monies from profits on his movies and by hiring out the facilities at Dickenson Road to other production companies such as [Hammer Horror](/wiki/Hammer_Horror "Hammer Horror") for filming [B-movies](/wiki/B-movie "B-movie"). After overcoming these difficulties, Blakely decided to retire when he reached 65, and handed control of Mancunian Films to his son Tom Blakely.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHunterPorter201263-7)

In the 1950s, the [growing reach television](/wiki/History_of_television "History of television") and the decline of cinema's audience size, led to many film studios being converted to for television broadcasts. In London, the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") acquired [Lime Grove Studios](/wiki/Lime_Grove_Studios "Lime Grove Studios") from [Gainsborough Pictures](/wiki/Gainsborough_Pictures "Gainsborough Pictures") in 1949, and [Ealing Studios](/wiki/Ealing_Studios "Ealing Studios") in 1955. Dickenson Road Studios was bought from Mancunian by the BBC in 1954, and it became the first regional [BBC Television](/wiki/BBC_Television "BBC Television") studio outside London.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERichards1997276-2)[[8]](#cite_note-itsahotun-history-8) Programmes made by the BBC at the studios included series starring comedian [Harry Worth](/wiki/Harry_Worth_(actor,_born_1917) "Harry Worth (actor, born 1917)") and variety programmes. The first episode of the [pop music](/wiki/British_popular_music "British popular music") television show *[Top of the Pops](/wiki/Top_of_the_Pops "Top of the Pops")* was broadcast from Dickenson Road Studio on 1 January 1964, presented by [Jimmy Savile](/wiki/Jimmy_Savile "Jimmy Savile") and opening with [The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") performing "[I Wanna Be Your Man](/wiki/I_Wanna_Be_Your_Man "I Wanna Be Your Man")". The studios remained the home of *Top of the Pops* until 1967, when the show moved to the larger facility at Lime Grove.[[9]](#cite_note-manchesterbeat-9) The Dickenson Road building was demolished in 1975 after operations were transferred to the BBC's new building at [New Broadcasting House](/wiki/New_Broadcasting_House_(Manchester) "New Broadcasting House (Manchester)") on [Oxford Road](/wiki/Oxford_Road,_Manchester "Oxford Road, Manchester").[[10]](#cite_note-genuki-10)

### Mancunian Films Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mancunian_Films&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mancunian Films Library")]

Mancunian Films Library, in storage at Kay Laboratories, was lost in a fire in 1980. Mike Blakeley, cameraman and grandson of John E. Blakeley, was reported to be attempting to find and restore all of Blakeley's films in 2003.[[5]](#cite_note-BBC20030217-5) Working with [CP Lee](/wiki/CP_Lee "CP Lee") of the [University of Salford](/wiki/University_of_Salford "University of Salford") in promoting the detailed history of the Mancunian Film Studio via film screenings and facts about Mancunian Films and its actors.[[8]](#cite_note-itsahotun-history-8)[[11]](#cite_note-itsahotun-11)[[12]](#cite_note-itsahotun-films-12)[[13]](#cite_note-itsahotun-stars-13)

## Selected filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mancunian_Films&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Selected filmography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Formby_in_Boots%21_Boots%21.jpg/250px-Formby_in_Boots%21_Boots%21.jpg)](/wiki/File:Formby_in_Boots!_Boots!.jpg)

[George Formby](/wiki/George_Formby "George Formby") and his wife [Beryl Formby](/wiki/Beryl_Formby "Beryl Formby") in Mancunian's 1934 production *[Boots! Boots!](/wiki/Boots!_Boots! "Boots! Boots!")*

* *[Boots! Boots!](/wiki/Boots!_Boots! "Boots! Boots!")* (1934)
* *[Off the Dole](/wiki/Off_the_Dole "Off the Dole")* (1935)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* *[Cup-tie Honeymoon](/wiki/Cup-tie_Honeymoon "Cup-tie Honeymoon")* (1948)
* *International Circus Review* (1948)
* *[Holidays with Pay](/wiki/Holidays_with_Pay "Holidays with Pay")* (1948)
* *Showground of the North* (1948)
* *[Somewhere in Politics](/wiki/Somewhere_in_Politics "Somewhere in Politics")* (1948)
* *What a Carry On* (1949)
* *[School for Randle](/wiki/School_for_Randle "School for Randle")* (1949)
* *[Over the Garden Wall](/wiki/Over_the_Garden_Wall_(1950_film) "Over the Garden Wall (1950 film)")* (1950)
* *Let's Have a Murder* (1950)
* *[Love's a Luxury](/wiki/Love%27s_a_Luxury "Love's a Luxury")* (1952)
* *[Those People Next Door](/wiki/Those_People_Next_Door "Those People Next Door")* (1952)
* *[It's a Grand Life](/wiki/It%27s_a_Grand_Life "It's a Grand Life")* (1953)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
